FOX-XP™ System: This is Aehr's flagship scalable, multi-wafer test and burn-in system. The market rewards those who identify important themes early; these are the themes we spend the most time researching:  
 
Photonics → $AAOI $MRVL $AEHR $LITE  
 
The photonics theme is becoming increasingly clear: as AI scales, the bottleneck is shifting from compute power to interconnect and data movement efficiency. 
 
AAOI, MRVL, AEHR, and LITE represent different layers of the photonics value chain—from high-speed optical modules and networking chips to testing infrastructure and core optical communication systems. The market is expanding from an “AI compute (GPU) narrative” to a broader re-rating of AI data flow efficiency. 
 
However, photonics is now entering a phase of accelerated expectation pricing, meaning volatility will be significantly higher than core leaders like NVDA and AVGO. Near-term performance will depend more on order flow and earnings execution than narrative expansion. 
 
The key question is: will photonics become the second major AI growth leg, or just a mid-cycle thematic extension? Do you prefer platform players like MRVL or high-beta names like AAOI?

These are the things we are focusing on most closely as we enter the second half of June.  
 
🔥 Bullish  $AMD $MU $NBIS $AEHR  
 
 Heading into the second half of June, this bullish watchlist is centered around one major theme: AI infrastructure expansion. AMD continues gaining traction in AI accelerators and servers, MU is a key beneficiary of rising HBM and memory demand, NBIS represents the growing AI compute and infrastructure buildout story, while AEHR benefits from increasing demand for semiconductor testing, power devices, and advanced packaging. 
 
The market is no longer focused solely on a few AI leaders. Capital is spreading across the entire ecosystem—from chips and memory to compute infrastructure and testing solutions. 
 
The real question is which companies can translate the AI narrative into accelerating earnings during the upcoming earnings season. Stories drive valuation in the short term, but execution drives long-term returns. 
 
If you could only hold one through year-end, would you choose AMD, MU, NBIS, or AEHR? Which AI beneficiary are you most bullish on right now?

 • Improves yield
 • Prevents failures
 • Enables scaling

As optical interconnects replace copper in AI infrastructure, AEHR quietly becomes a critical enabler behind the scenes

Lots of talk  going around that AeHR is the only game in town for wafer burn for Rubin.   Here is breakdown

AEHR Test Systems (FOX-XP) — Leader in high-power wafer-level burn-in (WLBI). Handles 18 wafers at once, up to 3,500W+ per wafer, full-wafer WaferPak contact, precise thermal control. Only production-proven for extreme AI processor needs. Strongest fit.
2.  Advantest — Dominates functional/parametric ATE (V93000). NVIDIA partner for Blackwell. High-power capable but optimized for short tests, not prolonged multi-wafer burn-in.
3.  Teradyne — Strong in high-throughput ATE (UltraFlex). Good for AI/GPU probing and stress, but lacks AEHR’s sustained high-power WLBI scale.
4.  Cohu — Offers burn-in and system-level testing (Island platform). Handles power/AI stress, mostly package-level or SLT, not full multi-wafer WLBI.
5.  OSATs (ASE/KYEC) — Handle backend burn-in for NVIDIA (e.g., Blackwell). Use custom chambers or adapted equipment

Assume Ultra Rubin ships ~2-3 million GPUs in calendar 2027 (first meaningful year, as an enhanced variant).
•  Total dies needed: ~2.5 million GPUs × 2 dies/GPU = 5 million dies.
•  Wafers needed: 5 million dies / 10 good dies per wafer = 500,000 wafers.
•  WLBI wafers (20% coverage): 100,000 wafers.
•  Systems needed for annual throughput: 100,000 wafers / ~75 wafers/system/month / 12 months = ~110 systems (cumulative to support 2027 ramp; some ordered in 2026 for qual/setup).
•  Phasing estimate:
 •  2026: Few to ~10-20 systems (eval/qual/initial production correlation).
 •  2027: ~50-100+ additional (ramp peak).
 •  2028+: Follow-on/maintenance.
•  Total cumulative FOX units (2026-2028): Dozens to low hundreds (~50-150 range), skewed lower if WLBI is more selective.

The battle of Rubin is supply chain and less cost saving.  

The economic incentive for WLBI has meaningfully increased from Blackwell to Rubin — higher per-unit cost, more integration layers, tighter cadence, and more severe consequences of field failures all push in that direction. But the decisive factor is that Nvidia didn’t adopt it for Blackwell not because the economics were wrong, but because the paradigm shift hasn’t happened yet in high-end GPU supply chains, and adding new process steps into an already constrained CoWoS pipeline carries real supply risk. Rubin Ultra (2027), with its four-chiplet architecture, 3.6 kW power envelope, and even higher complexity, may ultimately be the inflection point — but Rubin (2026) is the generation most likely to see serious qualification conversations begin, with Aehr’s paid evaluations with an unnamed “top-tier AI processor supplier” being a strong signal that those conversations are already underway.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Price is pulling back into the stacked green support rays after the prior push higher, and this is exactly where I want to see a higher low print. Those rays are drawn from the major low into the next higher low areas, then extended as rays, so when price returns to them you often get a clear read on whether the trend is still being defended. 
 
Right now the pullback looks more like a reset than a breakdown, because it is still sitting above the rising support structure while the big red downtrend ray overhead remains the main ceiling.  
 
Stochastic has cooled off into the lower zone and is trying to curl back up, and RSI is still holding the mid range, which fits the idea of momentum resetting rather than collapsing. 
 
I am starting to build a small position here, and I will only stay interested as long as price holds the green support rays on the weekly close.

$PSNL  What a great turnaround story this is!!  Probably the best turnaround story I know of is $AEHR:   Things were going well for AEHR but they had massive exposure to a single customer: Spansion -which was spun out of AMD & Fujitsu.   And they let Spansion purchase their FOX full-contact wafer testing equipment without actually paying for it.  BIG MISTAKE!!! Then... Spansion files for bankruptcy and they are not only out the money which is never repaid but they are out the equipment too - each FOX system being worth several million dollars.  So... the stock falls to about 40 cents and then recovers to about a buck but they are still losing lots of money as they try to rebuild.  They get so desperate that management actually passes the hat  around and collectively, they raise a million dollars just to keep the lights on.   Long story short - things got better and the stock actually made it to over $50/share at one time- now trades mid 20's.

$AEHR when I see short interest of any kind I tend to think it is institutional. Shorting is primarily a risk management tool which allows a firm to have higher long exposure than it otherwise would have; a firm can look at its net exposure and handle manage through volatility without panic covering its short. Normal people simply should not short - losses are theoretically unlimited, and one needs a real risk framework  
 
I am beginning to realizing there is, in at least some cases, real retail short exposure.  And retail short exposure - with the lack of risk management and panic covering that can ensue - is perhaps one of the greatest assets any sock can have
